perturbation.original_text: not to remove or export from the United States or re-export any such Proprietary Information or any direct product thereof except in compliance with all licenses and approvals required under applicable export laws and regulations, including without limitation, those of the U.S. Department of Commerce, and (v) not to copy or reverse engineer any such Proprietary Information.
perturbation.changed_text: not to remove or export from the United States or re-export any such Proprietary Information or any direct product thereof except in substantial compliance with what they reasonably understand to be applicable export laws and regulations, and (v) to refrain from overtly reverse engineering any such Proprietary Information.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'substantial compliance with what they reasonably understand' introduces ambiguity regarding export regulations. 'Refrain from overtly' creates uncertainty about the degree to which reverse engineering is allowed.
perturbation.law_explanation: EAR requires strict adherence to export controls, not 'substantial compliance' based on subjective understanding. The modification weakens the obligation, making enforcement difficult and potentially allowing unauthorized exports. Reverse engineering is also regulated depending on the nature of the information.

Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity by using phrases like 'substantial compliance with what they reasonably understand' and 'refrain from overtly,' which weakens the original obligation to strictly adhere to export laws and regulations. The original text mandated compliance with 'all licenses and approvals required under applicable export laws and regulations'. The Law Explanation states that the 'EAR requires strict adherence to export controls, not 'substantial compliance' based on subjective understanding.' Scraped Law Snippet 2 highlights the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774, implying that export activities are governed by strict regulations, and the modified text's ambiguity could lead to non-compliance, creating a legal contradiction as per the Perturbation Definitions.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party will turn over to the Disclosing Party all Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party and all documents or media containing any such Proprietary Information and any and all copies or extracts or derivatives thereof to the extent it is requested by either party in writing, except that a single copy may be retained for legal archival purposes, subject to protection and non-disclosure in accordance with the term of this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party will, to the best of its ability, return Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party and documents or media containing such information when reasonably requested in writing, except that a single copy may be retained if deemed necessary for archival purposes, subject to protection and non-disclosure in accordance with the term of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: 'To the best of its ability' weakens the obligation to return proprietary information. 'Reasonably requested' introduces subjectivity. 'If deemed necessary' allows the Receiving Party to decide if they need to archive it.
perturbation.law_explanation: The CTSA protects trade secrets, and failing to return them promptly and completely undermines this protection. The modifications allow for subjective interpretation, making it harder to prove misappropriation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.2. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.2. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to return proprietary information, introduces subjectivity in the request, and allows the Receiving Party to decide if they need to archive it. This contradicts the CTSA, as failing to return trade secrets promptly and completely undermines their protection. Snippet 1, CIV 3426.2(a), states that 'actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined,' indicating a legal expectation to prevent misappropriation. The changes in the contract facilitate potential misappropriation by creating ambiguities around the return of proprietary information, making the contract non-compliant and creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party is aware, and will advise its employees, consultants and agents who are informed of the matters that are the subject of this agreement, of the restrictions imposed by the United States securities laws on the purchase and sale of securities by any person who has received material, non-public information from the issuer of such securities and on the communication of such information to any other person when it is reasonably foreseeable that such other person is likely to purchase or sell such securities in reliance upon such information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party is generally aware, and will attempt to advise its employees, consultants and agents who are informed of the matters that are the subject of this agreement, of the general nature of securities laws regarding the purchase and sale of securities by any person who has received material, non-public information.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'is aware, and will advise' with 'is generally aware, and will attempt to advise' weakens the obligation to actively inform individuals about insider trading laws. Reducing the advice to the 'general nature' is also a weakening.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5 prohibit the use of manipulative or deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. The original text acknowledged the duty to inform relevant parties of these restrictions. The modification introduces ambiguity and weakens that duty, potentially failing to adequately prevent insider trading.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 15 / CHAPTER 2B / § 78j Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 15 USC 78j : Manipulative and deceptive devices Text contains those laws in effect on June 22, 2025 From Title 15-COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 2B-SECURITIES EXCHANGES Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments Effective Date Regulations §78j. Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The original text states a clear obligation to inform employees, consultants, and agents about securities laws, whereas the changed text weakens this obligation by stating the parties will 'attempt to advise' about the 'general nature' of these laws. This introduces ambiguity regarding the extent of the obligation, contradicting the intent of Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5 as the Law Explanation describes, which prohibit manipulative or deceptive devices related to securities. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 15 USC 78j, prohibits manipulative and deceptive devices which this weakening arguably facilitates by not ensuring informed parties. The modified text creates a potentially unenforceable obligation, which constitutes a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party will notify the Disclosing Party in writing immediately upon the occurrence of any such unauthorized release or other breach of which it is aware.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party will endeavor to notify the Disclosing Party in writing within a reasonable time frame following the occurrence of what the Receiving Party believes to be any unauthorized release or other potential breach of which it is reasonably certain.
perturbation.explanation: 'Endeavor to notify' weakens the obligation to report breaches. 'Reasonable time frame' introduces ambiguity. 'Believes to be' and 'reasonably certain' add subjective qualifiers.
perturbation.law_explanation: This California law requires timely notification of data breaches. The modified language introduces ambiguity and weakens the immediacy and certainty of reporting, conflicting with the legal requirement for prompt notification.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.8. PERSONAL DATA [1798 - 1798.78] ( Title 1.8 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 709. ) CHAPTER 1. Information Practices Act of 1977 [1798 - 1798.78] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 709. ) ARTICLE 7. Accounting of Disclosures [1798.25 - 1798.29] ( Article 7 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 709. ) 1798.29. (a) Any agency that owns or licenses computerized data that includes personal information shall disclose any breach of the security of the system following discovery or notification of the breach in the security of the data to any resident of California (1) whose unencrypted personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person, or, (2) whose encrypted personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person and the encryption key or security credential was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person and the agency that owns or licenses the encrypted information has a reasonable belief that the encryption key or security credential could
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Civil Code 1798.29(a) by weakening the obligation to report data breaches. The original text mandates immediate notification, whereas the modified text introduces subjective qualifiers such as 'endeavor to notify,' 'reasonable time frame,' 'believes to be,' and 'reasonably certain.' These changes introduce ambiguity and discretion, conflicting with the law's requirement for prompt notification following discovery or notification of the breach. The law snippet specifies 'shall disclose any breach of the security of the system following discovery or notification of the breach,' indicating a mandatory and timely action. The modified text undermines this legal obligation, making it less stringent and potentially delaying or preventing required notifications.
